free_text: The giant dipole resonances in 11C and 15O observed
with the reactions 10B(p,gamma) and 14N(p,gamma)15O
The Stanford FN-tandem Van de Graaff
accelerator provided proton beams from 2.2 to 19 MeV.
The gamma-rays were detected with a 24 cmx 24
cm NaI(Tl) crystal surrounded by a plastic
scintillator operated in anticoincidence. The use of
fast electronics makes efficient pile-up suppression
possible with negligible deadtime even in the
presence of high background. In an effort to reduce
background, radiations from sources other than the
target itself were reduced to a minimum. Only one
aperture was placed between the target and the
switching magnet which was located outside the target
room. The beam current striking this aperture one
meter in front of the target was monitored and
maintained at less than 0.2 % of the target current.
After traversing the thin targets the beam was
collected in a Faraday cup, surrounding by shielding,
about 4 m from the target.
No information on source of uncertainties.
VM
BP: Digitized Figs. 3,4,5,7,8. NACRE
data from subentry 3 replaced.
OS. REACTION corrected in subs.6-48
Fig.3, page 134.
Target consisted of three stacked,
self-supporting 10B foils made by electron gun
evaporation. Each  foil was about 100 microg/cm2 thick.
Cross section obtained from first coefficient of
Legendre fit to angular distribution
Top panel of Fig.5
BP. NACRE data replaced by Fig.5 data.
Fig.7, page 137.
The 14N gas was contained in a transmission cell
mounted in a 20 cm scattering chamber with an 0.16 cm
stainless steel wall.
Table 1, page 138.
Fig.4, page 135.
The target was made by evaporating
10B (about  150 microg/cm2) onto a 0.254 mm gold
backing. The target was  inserted into a glass "T" and
the Faraday cup was removed.
Angular distribution is plotted in units of l/Ao,
where Ao is obtained from the least-squares fit to
W(theta) = Ao[1 + Sum (an x Pn(cos theta))], n>0.
